common_name: Yellow Ground Orchid (Pubescent Spathoglottis, Hairy Ground Orchid)

planting.note: Plant pseudobulbs or divisions in spring in partial shade in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic soil; Spathoglottis pubescens is slightly less cold-tolerant than S. plicata but grows in similar conditions. The distinctive yellow to golden-yellow flowers distinguish it from the pink-purple S. plicata. Plant alongside S. plicata for a colorful mixed ground orchid bed. Divide every 3–4 years. The common name 'pubescens' refers to fine hairs on the flowers and plant surfaces. Non-toxic.

directions: Plant in partial shade in consistently moist, humus-rich, well-drained soil — Yellow Ground Orchid performs best in the filtered light of a shaded garden bed with regular moisture and organic-rich soil, conditions that approximate its native forest-margin habitat. Spathoglottis pubescens is a clump-forming terrestrial orchid native to southern China, India, the Himalayas, Thailand, Vietnam, and adjacent Southeast Asia in the Orchidaceae family, one of the most attractive Spathoglottis species for its distinctive flower color: unlike the rose-pink to magenta flowers of the more commonly grown S. plicata, S. pubescens produces flowers in clear, bright golden-yellow to canary-yellow with a darker orange-yellow lip — a warm, cheerful color that stands out particularly well against the green foliage of a shaded garden bed. The plant has a similar growth habit to S. plicata — oval pseudobulbs, pleated green leaves, and tall (18–30 inch) flower scapes bearing 5–15 flowers in succession — but the yellow coloration and the fine, soft hairs on the flowers and plant surfaces (the 'pubescens' — hairy) distinguish it from its pink relative. Planted together, yellow S. pubescens and pink S. plicata create an attractive bicolor ground orchid display that provides weeks of sequential bloom through the warm months. One of the finest terrestrial orchids for zone 9-12 shade gardens. Non-toxic.
sources: American Orchid Society — Spathoglottis pubescens cultivation notes
Royal Botanic Gardens Kew — Spathoglottis pubescens species profile
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Terrestrial Orchids for Florida shade gardens
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Ground Orchids for zone 9 Gulf Coast gardens

planting.note: Plant pseudobulbs or container-grown divisions in spring in partial to full shade in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic soil; Calanthe rosea is a deciduous species — its leaves die back in fall and the pseudobulbs rest through winter. Withhold water almost completely during dormancy (October–January) and resume as new growth starts in spring. Protect pseudobulbs from frost — zone 10 minimum for outdoor planting, zone 9 in containers. The delicate rose-pink flowers emerge in late fall to winter on leafless pseudobulbs. Non-toxic.

directions: Grow in partial to full shade in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, acidic soil during the active growing season — Rose Calanthe needs consistent moisture and shade to develop strong pseudobulbs, but requires a near-dry winter dormancy period to trigger the delicate flower display on the leafless pseudobulbs. Calanthe rosea is a deciduous terrestrial orchid native to the Philippines and adjacent Southeast Asia in the Orchidaceae family, closely related to the widely grown tropical deciduous calanthes and producing one of the most refined and delicate flower displays available from a terrestrial orchid. The plant grows large, egg-shaped pseudobulbs with broad, pleated, fresh-green leaves in the growing season (spring through fall); as temperatures cool in fall, the leaves yellow and drop, leaving the bare pseudobulbs. On these leafless pseudobulbs, arching flower spikes emerge in late fall to winter, bearing 10–20 small (1-inch) flowers in delicate shades of rose-pink to clear pink with a slightly frilled lip — the pure, soft color and graceful arching of the spike over the bare pseudobulbs creates a distinctly elegant display. The flowers are lightly fragrant. Calanthe rosea is grown in tropical orchid collections for both its ornamental flower quality and its interesting deciduous habit — the emergence of flower spikes from bare pseudobulbs with no leaves is a striking seasonal event. Non-toxic.
sources: American Orchid Society — Tropical deciduous Calanthe species cultivation guide
Royal Botanic Gardens Kew — Calanthe rosea species profile
Fairchild Tropical Botanic Garden — Calanthe species for tropical collections
ASPCA Animal Poison Control — Calanthe (non-toxic to pets)

common_name: White Burma Orchid (Snow-White Thunia, Phaius Thunia)

planting.note: Plant divisions or detached leafy stem sections in spring in partial shade in moist, humus-rich, well-drained orchid bark and soil mix; Thunia alba is a deciduous orchid — its tall, reed-like canes grow actively in spring and summer, flower at the tips in early summer, then shed their leaves in fall as the canes die back, leaving dormant cane bases. Keep nearly dry through winter. Requires cool to moderate winter temperatures (45–60°F) for best performance. The terminal clusters of large white flowers in June–July are spectacular. Propagate from stem cuttings or by removing offsets. Zone 9 in containers; outdoors zone 10+. Non-toxic.

directions: Grow in partial shade in moist, humus-rich, well-drained soil or orchid bark mix during the active growing season — White Burma Orchid grows vigorously in warm, humid conditions from spring through early fall and requires consistent moisture to develop its tall, leafy canes. Thunia alba is a deciduous terrestrial to semi-epiphytic orchid native to Myanmar (Burma), Yunnan Province of China, northeastern India, and Nepal in the Orchidaceae family, producing one of the most spectacular terminal flower displays of any terrestrial orchid relative to its size. The plant grows tall, upright, reed-like canes (2–4 feet) clothed in alternate, elliptical, gray-green leaves throughout the growing season. In early summer (June–July), the growing tip of the cane produces a large, pendant, multi-flowered inflorescence of 5–12 large (2–3 inch) flowers: pure white petals and sepals with a yellow-orange to orange, fringed lip decorated with purple veins — the combination of the pure white outer parts and the brightly colored, frilled, intricate lip creating a flower of considerable beauty. After blooming, the canes continue to grow slightly, then begin shedding leaves from the base upward as the plant enters dormancy. The leafless canes can be detached and rooted as cuttings — a convenient propagation method. A showy and relatively easy deciduous orchid for zone 9-11 with cool winters. Non-toxic.
sources: American Orchid Society — Thunia alba cultivation guide
Royal Botanic Gardens Kew — Thunia alba species profile
Royal Horticultural Society — Thunia cultivation notes
ASPCA Animal Poison Control — Thunia (non-toxic to pets)

planting.note: Grow mounted on cork bark or in a shallow basket with coarse bark medium in bright indirect to filtered direct light; Bulbophyllum lobbii is one of the easier large Bulbophyllum species for intermediate indoor conditions. The single large flower per spike emerges from the base of the pseudobulb and holds itself somewhat upright — the lateral sepals, which are the longest parts of the flower, rock gently in air movement, a motion that mimics an insect to attract pollinators. Maintain moderate humidity and water regularly. Blooms spring–summer. Protect from temperatures below 50°F. Non-toxic to pets.

directions: Grow mounted on cork or in a shallow basket with coarse bark in bright indirect to filtered direct light — Lobb's Bulbophyllum grows most vigorously when its roots can spread freely across a flat surface (mount) or in the shallow, free-draining conditions of a flat basket. Bulbophyllum lobbii is a sympodial, epiphytic orchid native to Thailand, Malaysia, Myanmar, Borneo, Sumatra, Java, and the Philippines in the Orchidaceae family, one of the finest and most readily available large-flowered Bulbophyllum species for tropical orchid collections. Bulbophyllum is the largest genus of orchids (over 2,000 species), and B. lobbii is considered one of the most ornamentally satisfying for its balance of large flower size, pleasing fragrance, and relative ease of cultivation. The plant produces round, ribbed pseudobulbs each with a single, broad, thick, leathery leaf. From the base of the pseudobulb, a single (occasionally 2) flower stem emerges carrying one large (3–4 inch) flower: creamy yellow to pale lemon-yellow with variable dark spotting in pink, purple, or reddish-brown on the petals, and long, tapering lateral sepals that project outward and downward. These lateral sepals are hinged at the base and rock freely back and forth in any air movement — a movement that mimics an insect in flight and is thought to attract pollinators. The flowers emit a pleasant, slightly musky sweet fragrance. Named for Thomas Lobb, the famous 19th-century plant collector. Non-toxic.
sources: American Orchid Society — Bulbophyllum lobbii cultivation guide
Royal Botanic Gardens Kew — Bulbophyllum lobbii species profile
Royal Horticultural Society — Bulbophyllum cultivation notes
ASPCA Animal Poison Control — Bulbophyllum (non-toxic to pets)

common_name: Bucket Orchid (Large Helmet Orchid, Macrantha Coryanthes)

planting.note: Grow in a large, open slatted basket with coarse bark — Coryanthes macrantha produces pendant flower spikes that must emerge through the bottom of the basket; solid pots prevent blooming. High humidity (70–80%+) is essential. Water daily in active growth; reduce slightly in winter. Requires warm temperatures (65–85°F) year-round. The extraordinary 'bucket' mechanism requires no action by the grower — flowers self-function to trap and release bees for pollination, but in cultivation outside the orchid's native range, hand-pollination with a toothpick is needed for seed set. An advanced collector's orchid requiring attention to its specific cultural needs.

directions: Grow in bright indirect to shade in a large open slatted basket with high humidity — Bucket Orchid is a demanding, specialized orchid best suited to advanced collectors with greenhouse or high-humidity conditions. Coryanthes macrantha is an epiphytic orchid native to Venezuela, Colombia, Trinidad, and Tobago in the Orchidaceae family, belonging to one of the most scientifically celebrated genera in all of botany for possessing what may be the most elaborate and sophisticated pollination mechanism in the plant kingdom. The lip of the Coryanthes flower is modified into an actual functioning bucket structure: a water-collecting, cup-shaped depression at the base of the complex lip assembly, which fills with a liquid secreted by the two glands above it. Male orchid bees (Eulaema, Eufriesea species) are attracted to the flower by an irresistible fragrance compound that the bees collect for use in mating displays. While gathering this compound from the front of the flower, male bees invariably slip and fall into the liquid-filled bucket. The bee cannot fly in the wet liquid and must crawl along the inner bucket surface toward the only exit — a narrow tube-shaped passage at one end — where it must squeeze past the column, collecting pollinia on its back in the process. When the same bee visits another Coryanthes flower and falls into its bucket and crawls out, the pollinia are precisely deposited on the stigma. The entire mechanism has the precision of engineered machinery. One of the most remarkable evolutionary achievements in nature, now available for cultivation by dedicated orchid enthusiasts.
sources: American Orchid Society — Coryanthes: Bucket Orchid biology and cultivation
Royal Botanic Gardens Kew — Coryanthes macrantha: bucket orchid pollination
Dodson, C.H. — Pollination of Coryanthes by orchid bees (Annals of the Missouri Botanical Garden)
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Advanced epiphytic orchids for tropical collections
